resume:
PRODUCT DESIGN INTERN CANDIDATE — UX / Interaction / Visual

SUMMARY
Final-year Interaction Design student seeking a product design internship where I can contribute to real shipped features. Over the last three years I have moved from purely visual work toward end-to-end product thinking, with a particular interest in onboarding flows, design systems, and research-informed iteration. I care about the why behind a screen as much as the pixels on it, and I am most energized when I get to talk to actual users, watch them struggle, and turn that struggle into a cleaner flow. The descriptions below are deliberately detailed because most of my work lives in process rather than in a single hero shot, and I would rather a reviewer understand how I think than just see a polished mockup with no story behind it.

EDUCATION
State University of Design and Technology — BFA, Interaction Design (expected next spring). Relevant coursework: Interaction Design Studio I–III, Design Research Methods, Information Architecture, Typography Systems, Prototyping with Code, Service Design, and Accessibility in Practice. Cumulative focus on bridging research and craft. Senior capstone in progress on accessible onboarding for first-time fintech users.

DESIGN EXPERIENCE

Campus Foodie — Student Project turned Pilot (most recent, roughly the last academic year)
Campus Foodie began as a course project in my Interaction Design Studio III class and grew into a small live pilot used by a few hundred students on campus. The product is a lightweight app for ordering from campus dining locations and skipping the physical line. I owned the onboarding and the order-tracking experience, and I collaborated with two other students who handled the menu browsing and the payment screens. My responsibilities spanned early discovery research, wireframing, a clickable prototype, two rounds of usability testing, a visual pass, and a redlined handoff to the two computer-science students who built the pilot. I ran six moderated usability sessions in a campus library study room, recruited entirely by flyers and a sign-up sheet, and I synthesized the findings into an affinity map that drove the second iteration. The single biggest change between versions was collapsing a four-step onboarding into a two-step flow after I watched four of six testers abandon at the dietary-preferences step; I moved that step to a later optional moment inside the app instead of forcing it up front. I also designed the live order-status component, including the empty, in-progress, ready, and picked-up states, and I wrote the microcopy for each. After the pilot launched I kept a lightweight feedback channel open and triaged incoming complaints into a simple backlog, the top item of which was confusion about pickup location, which I addressed by adding a map thumbnail and a plain-language landmark line to the ready state.

Northlight Studio — Freelance Visual Design (intermittent, over roughly two years before and during school)
Before I leaned fully into interaction design I took on freelance visual work for a small local studio that served restaurants, a yoga collective, and a couple of campus organizations. The work was mostly brand and marketing: logo refreshes, event posters, social templates, and the occasional one-page marketing site mocked up in Figma and handed to a developer. This is where I learned typographic discipline, grid systems, and how to take direction from a client who cannot always articulate what they want. I learned to present three directions instead of one, to name my rationale out loud, and to kill a design I was personally attached to when the feedback pointed elsewhere. I also learned the unglamorous parts: versioning files so a client revision does not destroy last week's approved work, exporting assets in the right formats, and writing a short rationale note so the developer does not guess at spacing. Although this work was visual rather than product, it built the craft floor I now stand on.

The Reading Corner — Volunteer App Concept (early, my sophomore year, predates the freelance work above in terms of when I started it)
The Reading Corner was an unpaid concept project for a local nonprofit literacy program. They wanted to imagine a volunteer-scheduling app but had no budget to build it, so a classmate and I produced a research-backed concept and a high-fidelity prototype they could use to apply for a grant. I conducted four stakeholder interviews with program coordinators, mapped their current paper-and-phone scheduling process into a service blueprint, and identified the two moments that caused the most no-shows. The prototype focused on a reminder-and-confirmation loop and a simple coordinator dashboard. Because there was no engineering partner, the project ended at a validated prototype and a short recommendations deck rather than a shipped product, but it was my first real taste of framing a problem from research rather than from a brief.

SKILLS AND TOOLS
Figma (primary, including auto-layout, components, and variants), FigJam for synthesis, basic HTML and CSS for understanding constraints, a little JavaScript from my Prototyping with Code course, Principle and simple Figma prototyping for motion, and Maze for unmoderated tests. Research methods I have actually run: moderated usability testing, stakeholder interviews, affinity mapping, service blueprinting, and lightweight survey design. I am comfortable with accessibility fundamentals — color contrast, focus order, hit-target sizing, and writing alt text — though I would not yet call myself an accessibility specialist.

HOW I LIKE TO WORK
I like starting from the user's actual words, not from a feature list. I prototype early and rough so that I am testing ideas rather than defending finished art. I keep a tidy file because future-me and the engineer both have to live in it. I ask for critique often and try not to take it personally. I am aware that as an intern my job is to learn fast, ask good questions, and make the people around me a little less busy rather than a little more busy.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
An internship on a product team where research and craft both matter, where I can own a small but real slice end to end, and where someone more senior will tell me honestly when I am wrong so I can get better quickly.
jd: We are hiring a Product Design Intern to join a consumer product team for a full cycle. Reporting to a Senior Product Designer, you will own a small, real feature slice end to end — from discovery research through wireframes, prototyping, usability testing, and developer handoff. You will run moderated usability sessions, synthesize findings, and iterate. We expect interns to be precise about their own process and timeline: when research happened relative to design, what changed between iterations and why, and what they personally did versus what the team did. The ideal candidate has a portfolio that shows process, can walk through a project chronologically without contradicting themselves, and is hungry for direct feedback.

history.q: Tell me about the project you're proudest of and your specific role on it.
Let's take Campus Foodie. Who came up with the original idea, and what problem were you solving?
You said you ran six usability sessions. Walk me through how you recruited and what you asked.
What was the single biggest design change between your first and second version?
Help me get the sequence straight: did the usability sessions happen before or after you built the clickable prototype?
And the affinity map — was that built from the sessions, or before them?
When did you collapse the four-step onboarding to two steps — which round was that?
So the dietary-preferences abandonment — you saw that in testing, then changed the flow. Which testing round surfaced it?
After the pilot launched, the pickup-location confusion — was that from pre-launch testing or post-launch feedback?
Let me replay your timeline back to you and you tell me if I have it right.
Just walk me through the project start to finish, in order, one more time — discovery, prototype, testing, the onboarding change, launch, and the pickup fix.

candidate_last_answer: Sure. So we started by building the clickable prototype first, and then I did discovery research to figure out what users wanted. The six usability sessions came after I'd already collapsed the onboarding to two steps — actually, wait, the onboarding change came out of the sessions, because that's where I saw people abandon at the dietary step. The affinity map I made before the sessions to plan them, but it's also what I built from the session findings to drive iteration two. And the pickup-location confusion, that one I caught in the usability tests, which is why I added the map thumbnail before we launched the pilot — although I also said earlier it came from post-launch feedback. Honestly the order kind of blurs together because it all happened over one semester.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: the order kind of blurs together

expected-yield: a single non-contradictory chronological sequence — whether discovery research preceded the prototype, which usability round produced the onboarding change, and whether the pickup-location fix was pre-launch or post-launch — resolving the conflicting orderings she gave
